ocr.text: In recent years, the analysis of transportation efficiency and its environmental impact has become increasingly important,
particularly in urban settings. The data presented in Table 1 provides a comprehensive overview of key performance metrics
related to bus operations over several years, specifically focusing on kilometers traveled (km) and fuel consumption measured
in liters per kilometer (lit/km). Notably, the table includes data from 1987, 2002, 2005, and 2007, allowing for a longitudinal
comparison of operational efficiency and changes in separation conditions.
Type of vehicles  V  RD  AV
(km)  (lit/km)
Private Car  2400000  43  0.1
Motorcycle  560000  40  0.04
Requirements  ESPGAN [32]  LSRO [31]  Atkinson [33]  Rigo [34]
GC separation conditions
Injection  Multimode (PTV) injection, splitless mode
Injection volume  2 μl
Carrier gas  Helium; 1 ml/min (50 min), then 2 ml/min (until the end of the analytical run)
Column  Agilent DB-EUPAH (60 m × 250 mm × 0.25 μm)

Oven temperature programme  50 °C (0.8 min), ramp 45 °C/min to 200 °C, ramp 2.5 °C/min to 225 °C, ramp 5 °C/min to 266 °C, ramp 14 °C/min to 300 °C, ramp 10 °C/min up to 320 °C (48 min). Total run time 74.762 min
The findings indicate a significant evolution in bus performance, with the operational metrics revealing trends that may
correlate with advancements in technology and changes in regulatory standards. Furthermore, the table highlights the
importance of monitoring calcium levels expressed in mg/kg/day, which is crucial for assessing the environmental impact of
transportation systems. The optimal range for calcium intake, as indicated, suggests a focus on maintaining health and safety
standards within the operational framework of public transportation. This data serves as a vital resource for policymakers and
transportation planners aiming to enhance the sustainability of bus services in urban environments.
